title: Chapter 3 · Mathematical Foundations
text:
Exercises
16 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 3.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 3.1 — Type-check each expression against the notation hierarchy, marking it well-formed or ill-formed and correcting the ill-formed: (a) \(\mathbf{A}\x + \uc\); (b) \(\x + \mathbf{A}\); (c) \(\x^{\!\top}\mathbf{A}\x\); (d) \(\Top \le \Xc\); (e) \(d(\x, \Top\x')\); (f) \(\rank(\x)\).
Exercise 3.2 — Give one enterprise mapping that is injective but not surjective, one surjective but not injective, and one bijective, drawing all three from reporting, transformation, or diagnosis contexts, and state in one sentence each what the property means for its context.
Exercise 3.3 — Explain, with two concrete operators, why transformation operators are defined with explicit domains rather than as total maps, and what modeling error the total-map convention would smuggle in.
Exercise 3.4 — For each situation, argue in two or three sentences whether a deterministic or stochastic model is the appropriate regime (Table (see book)): (a) capacity planning one quarter ahead in a utility; (b) sizing a liquidity buffer against funding shocks; (c) sequencing the workstreams of an ERP migration; (d) pricing a multi-year technology bet.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 3.5 — Prove: for any mapping \(f: \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) and subsets \(\mathcal{T}_1, \mathcal{T}_2 \subseteq \mathcal{B}\), \(f^{-1}(\mathcal{T}_1 \cap \mathcal{T}_2) = f^{-1}(\mathcal{T}_1) \cap f^{-1}(\mathcal{T}_2)\), and show by counterexample that the analogous identity for images, \(f(\mathcal{S}_1 \cap \mathcal{S}_2) = f(\mathcal{S}_1) \cap f(\mathcal{S}_2)\), can fail.
Exercise 3.6 — Verify the monoid axioms of Theorem (see book)(i) in detail for total operators, and exhibit a natural enterprise operator that is not injective, explaining which distinct states it merges and why the merger is economically irreversible.
Exercise 3.7 — Show that the bound \(L_2 L_1\) of Theorem (see book)(ii) is tight for linear operators (equality for suitable \(\Top_1\), \(\Top_2\)) and strict for a pair of your construction.
Exercise 3.8 — Establish the sharp sandwich constants between \(\norm{\cdot}_1\) and \(\norm{\cdot}_\infty\) on \(\R^n\): \(\norm{\x}_\infty \le \norm{\x}_1 \le n \norm{\x}_\infty\), identifying the vectors attaining each bound.
Exercise 3.9 — Complete the proof of Proposition (see book): verify each metric axiom for \(d_{\mathbf{W}}\) line by line, and determine whether \(d_{\mathbf{W}}\) remains a metric when \(\mathbf{W}\) is (a) diagonal with one zero entry, (b) a general invertible matrix.
Exercise 3.10 ★ —  For affine operators \(\Top_i\x = \mathbf{A}_i\x + \mathbf{b}_i\) (\(i = 1, 2\)) on \(\R^n\), derive the exact condition under which \(\Top_1 \circ \Top_2 = \Top_2 \circ \Top_1\), separating a matrix condition from a vector condition.
Exercise 3.11 ★ —  For Example (see book): verify the stated eigenvalues, compute \(\rho(\mathbf{A})\), and determine the steady state \(\bar{\x}\) under constant investment \(u_k \equiv \bar{u} = 10\) by solving \(\bar{\x} = \mathbf{A}\bar{\x} + \mathbf{B}\bar{u}\).
Exercise 3.12 — In Theorem (see book), replace the one-hot embedding of a categorical attribute with an arbitrary injective assignment of distinct real numbers to categories.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 3.13 — (With AXIOM-03 or the Chapter 3 notebook.
Exercise 3.14 — (With AXIOM-03 or the Chapter 3 notebook.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 3.15 — Extend your enterprise state vector from Exercise 2.
Exercise 3.16 ★ —  Model a merger of two enterprises with state spaces \(\Xc^{A} \subseteq \R^{n_A}\) and \(\Xc^{B} \subseteq \R^{n_B}\) as a transformation operator on the product space \(\Xc^{A} \times \Xc^{B}\) into a combined space \(\Xc^{AB}\).

title: Chapter 4 · Enterprise Modeling Principles
text: Volume I, Chapter 4 · Taught in Week 2 · Lab seed 26104

title: Chapter 4 · Enterprise Modeling Principles
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Explain the role of enterprise models as instruments--- purpose-built representations whose value lies in the questions they render answerable---and state the modeling contract every DCT model must declare.Distinguish conceptual, logical, mathematical, and computational models, and trace a single enterprise question through the full chain from concept to code.Apply abstraction and decomposition formally: construct abstraction maps, identify their fibers, and test the fiber-invariance condition under which coarse models are dynamically legitimate.Evaluate model validity and consistency, distinguishing verification from validation, stating what a validation record can and provably cannot certify, and explaining why transformation modeling necessarily exceeds its validation domain.Construct enterprise models suitable for quantitative analysis: declared attributes, units, measurement error, assumption classes, validation domain, and lifecycle plan.

title: Chapter 4 · Enterprise Modeling Principles
text:

title: Chapter 4 · Enterprise Modeling Principles
text:
Exercises
16 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 4.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 4.1 — Take the enterprise question “can Meridian fund the digital program without breaching leverage 4.
Exercise 4.2 — State the precise content that Theorem (see book) gives to each half of “all models are wrong, but some are useful,” and explain why the theorem makes the aphorism a consequence of Proposition 1.
Exercise 4.3 — Classify each of the following under the assumption classes of Table (see book): (a) “competitor entry is excluded”; (b) “retraining converts 3 (c)”the capabil…
Exercise 4.4 — A consultant reports: “the model reproduced the last five years with 2 are reliable.
Exercise 4.5 — Give one question about your own enterprise that is answerable in a coarse (\(L_2\)-style) model and one that Theorem (see book)(ii) renders inexpressible there, and state the minimal representation amendment that would admit the second.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 4.6 — Prove the unproved direction details of Theorem (see book): that the constructed \(\hat f'\) is the unique commuting transition, and that trajectory agreement holds for all horizons by explicit induction.
Exercise 4.7 — Carry out the repair of Example (see book): take the enlarged \(L_2\) state \((H, s)\) with \(s = h_s / H\), derive its exact dynamics from the fine model, and verify fiber-invariance of the enlarged aggregation directly.
Exercise 4.8 ★ —  For \(\hat f(\x) = \mathbf{A}\x\) and \(\alpha(\x) = \mathbf{P}\x\) with \(\mathbf{P}\) of full row rank, show that when \(\mathbf{A}\ker\mathbf{P} \subseteq \ker\mathbf{P}\) the unique coarse map is \(\hat f'(\x') = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{P}^{+}\x'\) with \(\mathbf{P}^{+}\) any right inverse of \(\mathbf{P}\), and that the expression is independent of the right inverse chosen.
Exercise 4.9 — In the proof of Proposition (see book), the constructed \(\hat f\) was arbitrary off the chosen fiber.
Exercise 4.10 ★ —  (Approximate aggregation.
Exercise 4.11 — Make Theorem (see book) concrete: on \(\Xc = [0, 10]\) with a record exciting only \([2, 5]\), exhibit two explicitly formulated one-dimensional models agreeing exactly on \([2, 5]\) and differing by at least \(\Delta = 3\) at \(x = 8\), and compute a validation discrepancy to confirm indistinguishability.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 4.12 — Write the full modeling contract (one to two pages) for the enterprise state vector you built in Exercise 3.
Exercise 4.13 — Design the test that Meridian’s CFO should run before trusting the consolidated (group-level) model for the transformation decision: which fibers does consolidation create, which known couplings cross them, and what data would detect fiber-splitting?
Exercise 4.14 ★ —  Conduct the domain audit of Example (see book) for a transformation you know: characterize the excited domain of the available record along four or more dimensions, locate the program’s trajectory relative to it, and annotate three off-domain conclusions with the structural assumptions that carry each, assessing how each assumption could be independently credentialed.


Part D — Enterprise applications

title: Chapter 5 · Enterprise State Representation
text: Volume I, Chapter 5 · Taught in Week 3 · Lab seed 26105

title: Chapter 5 · Enterprise State Representation
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Define an enterprise state mathematically, including the canonical minimal state as the enterprise's future-relevant memory (Theorem (see book)).Construct enterprise state vectors with declared variables, units, types, and measurement conventions.Differentiate state variables from enterprise parameters, and explain why the boundary between them is horizon-relative.Represent enterprises within multidimensional state spaces and read their geometry: admissibility, boundaries, and binding constraints.Interpret enterprise trajectories, in continuous and discrete time, including their sampling relationship and concatenation algebra.Identify feasible and infeasible enterprise states, and compute feasible regions as constraint intersections.Explain enterprise equilibrium, classify its stability informally, and state existence conditions for equilibria.Define enterprise configuration mathematically and translate configuration questions into vector-space geometry.Analyze enterprise state transitions, including the flow map that links continuous evolution to discrete epochs.Prepare enterprise representations for the transformation operator theory of Chapter 6.

title: Chapter 5 · Enterprise State Representation
text:
Exercises
18 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 5.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 5.1 — Using the touchstone “the state is the memory the future requires,” argue for or against state membership of each of the following, in two or three sentences each: (a) cumulative historical R&D spend; (b) the current patent portfolio; (c) last year’s reported earnings; (d) the CEO’s remaining contract term; (e) a concluded litigation’s outcome.
Exercise 5.2 — Give two quantities from your own enterprise that are parameters at a one-year horizon and state variables at a ten-year horizon, and one quantity for which the promotion has already happened in your organization’s planning practice.
Exercise 5.3 — Explain, citing Theorem (see book)(iii) and Example (see book), why a covenant model written with \(x_2 < 4.5\) instead of \(x_2 \le 4.5\) is not a harmless stylistic choice.
Exercise 5.4 — Distinguish an enterprise equilibrium from an enterprise target, and give one example where they coincide and one where a target is deliberately set at a non-equilibrium state.
Exercise 5.5 — In your own words: what exactly does Theorem (see book) make non-arbitrary about “the state,” and what does it leave as a modeling commitment?


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 5.6 — Prove the unproved refinements of Proposition (see book): that an arbitrary (possibly infinite) family of convex sets has convex intersection, and that the binding set \(\mathcal{B}(\x)\) is upper hemicontinuous in the following elementary sense: if \(g_j(\x) < 0\), then \(j \notin \mathcal{B}(\x')\) for all \(\x'\) sufficiently near \(\x\).
Exercise 5.7 — Prove that concatenation of discrete trajectories is associative where defined (Proposition (see book)(ii)), and show that the set of trajectories with concatenation forms a category whose objects are states—identifying identities and composition.
Exercise 5.8 ★ —  Carry out the construction of Theorem (see book) on a miniature behavior: outputs in \(\{0, 1\}\), decisions in \(\{a, b\}\), no environment, with \(\beta\) defined by “output 1 exactly when the history contains at least two \(b\)’s.
Exercise 5.9 ★ —  Complete the continuation theory of Theorem (see book)(ii): show that the local solution extends to a maximal interval, and that if the maximal interval is bounded, the solution leaves every compact subset of the Lipschitz region.
Exercise 5.10 — Derive from (see book) the a posteriori bound \(\norm{\x_k - \x^{\ast}} \le \frac{L}{1 - L} \norm{\x_k - \x_{k-1}}\), and explain its practical advantage over the a priori bound in monitoring a running transformation program.
Exercise 5.11 — Construct a continuous map of \([0, 1]^2\) into itself with exactly three equilibria, verify the hypotheses of Theorem (see book)(ii), and classify each equilibrium’s stability by linearization at the fixed points (using the spectral test of Table (see book)).
Exercise 5.12 — Prove that the sampled map \(\Phi_\Delta\) of Proposition (see book)(iii) inherits the Lipschitz property from \(f\) on a compact invariant region, with constant at most \(e^{L\Delta}\), using Gr"onwall’s inequality (state and prove the inequality’s elementary integral form as part of your answer).


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 5.13 — (With AXIOM-05 or the Chapter 5 notebook.
Exercise 5.14 — (With AXIOM-05 or the Chapter 5 notebook.
Exercise 5.15 ★ —  (With the Chapter 5 notebook.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 5.16 — Produce the Table (see book)-style declaration for your Exercise 4.
Exercise 5.17 — Identify the strongest candidate for an operating equilibrium of your enterprise (or a unit of it), state the balancing mechanisms that would make its period map contractive, and estimate—from one observed period-to-period change and a guessed \(L\)—the convergence budget (see book) to within tolerance of steady state.
Exercise 5.18 — Write a one-page memo in the voice of Example (see book)’s CFO: name the wall your growth trajectory will meet first, the constraint type it instantiates (Table (see book)), the regime handover you must design for the boundary, and the validity horizon of the current expansive model per Theorem (see book)’s Scope and Limits.

title: Chapter 6 · Enterprise Transformation Operators
text: Volume I, Chapter 6 · Taught in Week 3 · Lab seed 26106

title: Chapter 6 · Enterprise Transformation Operators
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Define Enterprise Transformation Operators mathematically, as declared partial self-maps on the enterprise state space.Distinguish deterministic from stochastic transformations by the single-valuedness of their event families, and know where each is treated.Represent enterprise evolution using transformation mappings, including the equivalence between operator application and system transition (Theorem (see book)).Analyze operator composition and sequencing, with the domain algebra that governs when programs chain.Define transformation domains and codomains, and use domains as the mathematical carrier of preconditions.Interpret transformation trajectories as orbits of operator words, and identify the sustainable core from which a playbook is indefinitely repeatable (Theorem (see book)).Evaluate transformation feasibility, including mid-program feasibility and its failure modes.Explain operator invertibility, and why irreversibility is the enterprise default.Analyze transformation stability, including the propagation of implementation error through iterated and composed operators (Theorem (see book)).Prepare transformation operators for the dynamic-systems integration of Chapter 7.

title: Chapter 6 · Enterprise Transformation Operators
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 6.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 6.1 — Give two distinct enterprise actions sharing one transformation mapping (Definition (see book)), and one analysis for which the distinction matters and one for which it does not.
Exercise 6.2 — For five real transformation actions from an enterprise you know, fill a row each of Table (see book): type, plausible mathematical form, and the domain condition practice would call “readiness.
Exercise 6.3 — A program office claims its operating-model program “can reach any target configuration the board specifies.
Exercise 6.4 — Why does the identity operator belong in every repertoire, and what role does it play in (a) the monoid structure, (b) program appraisal, (c) the reachable set \(R_k(\x_0)\)?
Exercise 6.5 — Classify each event family as deterministic or stochastic per Proposition (see book), one sentence each: (a) a price change with posted schedule; (b) a divestiture at auction; (c) a system migration with a tested cutover script; (d) a key-hire attempt; (e) a regulatory filing with discretionary review.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 6.6 — Prove the domain-associativity formula of Proposition (see book) for words of arbitrary finite length \(K\) by induction, expressing \(\operatorname{dom}(\Top_{K} \circ \cdots \circ \Top_1)\) as \(K\) nested membership conditions.
Exercise 6.7 ★ —  Complete Theorem (see book)(iii): show that a composite of continuous maps on closed domains is continuous on its (closed) chained domain, and construct an example with \(\operatorname{dom}\Top\) open in which \(\Xc_{\infty}(\Top)\) fails to be closed.
Exercise 6.8 — Show the bound (see book) is sharp: for each \(L > 0\) and \(\varepsilon > 0\), exhibit scalar operators \(\Top\x = L\x\) and \(\Top'\x = L\x + \varepsilon\) achieving equality at every \(k\).
Exercise 6.9 — Rerun the proof of Theorem (see book)(iii) when only \(\Top\) is assumed contractive and \(\Top'\) merely has some fixed point \(\x'^{\ast}\): does the bound survive?
Exercise 6.10 — Prove the minimality clause of Theorem (see book)(i) in detail, and show by example that \(R(\x_0)\) can be strictly smaller than the set of states reachable when domain conditions are ignored—quantifying, on your example, the “feasibility gap.
Exercise 6.11 ★ —  Characterize the idempotent affine operators on \(\R^n\): derive the exact conditions on \((\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{b})\) under which \(\Top\x = \mathbf{A}\x + \mathbf{b}\) satisfies \(\Top \circ \Top = \Top\), describe the fixed-point set geometrically, and interpret idempotence as the “one-and-done” program of Table (see book): which enterprise actions plausibly have it, and which merely claim it?
Exercise 6.12 — Formalize the tolerance repair of Theorem (see book)(iii): show that for the repertoire \(\Top\x = \x/2\) on \([0, 1]\), the tolerance target \(\mathcal{G}_\varepsilon = [0, \varepsilon]\) is attained in exactly \(\lceil \log_2(1/\varepsilon) \rceil\) steps from \(\x_0 = 1\), and state the general principle relating tolerance to shortest-path length under a contraction.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 6.13 — (With AXIOM-06 or the Chapter 6 notebook.
Exercise 6.14 — (With AXIOM-06 or the Chapter 6 notebook.
Exercise 6.15 — (With AXIOM-06 or the Chapter 6 notebook.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 6.16 — Draft your enterprise’s repertoire table: five to eight operators with declared effects, domains (Table (see book) types), Lipschitz class where estimable, and reversibility premium (finite estimate or “infinite: commitment”).
Exercise 6.17 — For a merger or major partnership you know (or a hypothetical for Meridian’s Digital Solutions), write the seam memo of Example (see book): the three-operator word, the two seam conditions \(\operatorname{ran}\Top_i \subseteq \operatorname{dom}\Top_{i+1}\) spelled as checkable predicates, the environmentally-carried effects flagged per Theorem (see book)(ii), and the gate at which the reversibility premium becomes infinite.
Exercise 6.18 — Apply Example (see book) to a three-year transformation you know: augment the state with a change budget, cost each operator, verify prefix-sum feasibility of the intended word, determine whether the plan is a strategy or a trade (core empty or not), and if a trade, specify the minimal regeneration operator that would convert it.
Exercise 6.19 — For Example (see book) with \(\gamma = 0.8\), \(q_{\min} = 0.3\), \(\x_0 = (x_4, q) = (41, 0.9)\): iterate the closed-loop operator six periods, verify the trajectory respects the domain throughout, identify the operator’s Lipschitz class in each coordinate, and state which regime of Theorem (see book) governs its execution-error budget.

title: Chapter 7 · Dynamic Enterprise Systems
text: Volume I, Chapter 7 · Taught in Week 4 · Lab seed 26107

title: Chapter 7 · Dynamic Enterprise Systems
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Define a Dynamic Enterprise System mathematically, as the integration of state representation (ESA) and transformation operators (ETA) under an explicit time semantics.Formulate deterministic enterprise state equations in continuous and discrete time, with declared system class (autonomous, time-invariant, linear).Model enterprise evolution through time, including the superposition of inherited momentum and program contribution in the linear regime.Distinguish continuous-time and discrete-time enterprise systems, and convert between them exactly in the linear time-invariant case.Analyze enterprise trajectories: existence, uniqueness, continuous dependence on initial conditions, and invariance of the feasible region.Interpret equilibrium and steady-state behavior, including input-shifted equilibria and multiplicity.Evaluate enterprise stability by the spectral tests and by Lyapunov's direct method.Apply feedback within enterprise dynamics: gain design, constructive pole placement, and the overcorrection phenomenon.Analyze enterprise responses to external inputs, and determine which internal states are reconstructible from observed outputs.Prepare deterministic enterprise systems for the stochastic generalization of Chapter 8.

title: Chapter 7 · Dynamic Enterprise Systems
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 7.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 7.1 — Classify each of the following along all four axes of Table (see book), one line each: (a) a quarterly covenant-compliance model; (b) an intraday liquidity model; (c) a platform-adoption model with network effects; (d) a model of demand under an announced regulatory phase-in.
Exercise 7.2 — For your own enterprise, name two decision inputs, two environment inputs, one delivered output, and two reported outputs; state which reported output is a mixture of state coordinates rather than a single coordinate, and why that matters for Theorem (see book)(iii).
Exercise 7.3 — Distinguish “equilibrium of the autonomous dynamics” from “steady state under a held policy,” using (see book); give a Meridian instance where the two differ and explain which one a board target should reference.
Exercise 7.4 — A colleague discretizes a continuous LTI treasury model by Euler’s method with \(\Delta\) one quarter.
Exercise 7.5 — Give one enterprise scenario for each row of Table (see book), distinct from the instances printed there, and state for each which test you would run.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 7.6 — From the continuous-dependence bound (see book), derive the forecastable-horizon formula \(t_{\max} \approx (1/L)\ln( \mathrm{tol}/\mathrm{err})\) for tolerance \(\mathrm{tol}\) and initial measurement error \(\mathrm{err}\); evaluate it for Meridian’s capability coordinate (\(\pm 5\) points per Table 5.
Exercise 7.7 — Prove the generalized Gr"onwall inequality with time-varying rate: if \(\varphi(t) \le c + \int_0^t \ell(s)\varphi(s)\, \mathrm{d}s\) with \(\ell \ge 0\) continuous, then \(\varphi(t) \le c\,\exp\bigl(\int_0^t \ell(s)\,\mathrm{d}s\bigr)\).
Exercise 7.8 — Prove the diagonalizable case of Theorem (see book)(i) in full: for \(\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{V}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{V}^{-1}\), show \(\norm{\x_k} \le \kappa(\mathbf{V})\,\rho(\mathbf{A})^{k} \norm{\x_0}\) with \(\kappa(\mathbf{V}) = \norm{\mathbf{V}} \norm{\mathbf{V}^{-1}}\), and explain the enterprise meaning of a large eigenbasis condition number (near-parallel modes: transient growth before decay).
Exercise 7.9 ★ —  For the planar rotation \(\mathbf{A} = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}\cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]\), prove Lyapunov stability of the origin directly from Definition (see book) (exhibit \(\delta(\varepsilon)\)), prove that asymptotic stability fails, and exhibit the Jordan pair at \(\rho = 1\) whose instability completes the theorem’s “inconclusive” clause.
Exercise 7.10 — Re-derive (see book) by splitting \(\x_k = \x_k^{\mathrm{free}} + \x_k^{\mathrm{forced}}\) from the start: define the two sequences by their own recursions, prove each satisfies it, and prove the sum satisfies the original—superposition as three small inductions.
Exercise 7.11 — A covenant package reports \(\mathbf{y}_k = \mathbf{C}\x_k\) with \(\mathbf{C}\) selecting (leverage, liquidity) from a four-coordinate state (liquidity, leverage, capability, technology) whose \(\mathbf{A}\) couples capability into liquidity with a one-period lag but couples technology into nothing financial for two periods.
Exercise 7.12 ★ —  Carry out Theorem (see book)(iii) for \(n = 2\) from scratch: take \(\mathbf{A} = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}0 & 1 \\ -a_2 & -a_1\end{smallmatrix} \bigr]\), \(\mathbf{B} = [0, 1]^{\!\top}\), desired eigenvalues \(\{0.5, 0.6\}\); compute \(\mathbf{K}\), verify the closed-loop characteristic polynomial by direct expansion, and then repeat with desired eigenvalues \(\{0.5 \pm 0.3i\}\) to confirm complex placement.
Exercise 7.13 — Derive the general overcorrection threshold for the scalar system with delay: \(x_{k+1} = a x_k + b u_{k-1}\) (the decision acts one period late), under \(u_k = -k x_k\).


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 7.14 — (With AXIOM-07 or the Chapter 7 notebook.
Exercise 7.15 — (With AXIOM-07 or the Chapter 7 notebook.
Exercise 7.16 — (With AXIOM-07 or the Chapter 7 notebook.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 7.17 — Write the dynamic declaration (Table (see book) row memberships, with justification and expiry conditions) for the enterprise model you built in Exercises 4.
Exercise 7.18 — Write a one-page gain audit of one management control loop you know (pricing, hiring, inventory, credit approval): the deviation variable, the instrument, the estimated \((a, b)\), the operating gain and its Table (see book) regime, the computed \(k_{\mathrm{crit}}\), and one observed episode reinterpreted through the chart.
Exercise 7.19 — For a perimeter-changing transformation you know (divestiture, carve-out, acquisition), sketch the hybrid timeline of Example (see book): pre-dynamics, jump operator with feasibility check, post-dynamics, and the three-item discipline (jump feasibility, post-spectrum re-diagnosis, revalidation) with the evidence each item would require.

title: Chapter 8 · Stochastic Enterprise Dynamics
text: Volume I, Chapter 8 · Taught in Week 4 · Lab seed 26108

title: Chapter 8 · Stochastic Enterprise Dynamics
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Explain the need for stochastic enterprise models, and name the modeling channels through which uncertainty enters.Distinguish deterministic from stochastic enterprise dynamics, including the shift in what evolves deterministically---the state versus its distribution.Define enterprise stochastic processes on a declared probability space, with adaptedness and the Markov property.Represent enterprise uncertainty mathematically: random environments, noise sequences, and their declared distributions.Formulate stochastic enterprise state equations in discrete time and as stochastic differential equations.Interpret diffusion and jump processes as the two canonical shapes of enterprise randomness---continuous accumulation and discrete events.Analyze stochastic enterprise trajectories: sample paths, fan charts, and the distinction between the mean path and typical paths.Understand probabilistic state evolution through transition kernels and the Chapman--Kolmogorov structure.Relate uncertainty to enterprise decision-making: the flaw of averages, uncertainty amplification through operators, and Monte Carlo estimation with honest error bars.Prepare the probabilistic foundation for the risk and resilience architecture of Chapter 12 and the stochastic optimization of Volume II.

title: Chapter 8 · Stochastic Enterprise Dynamics
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 8.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 8.1 — Classify five uncertainties facing an enterprise you know into the rows of Table (see book), naming for each the model channel and the canonical shape (diffusion-like or jump-like) with one sentence of justification.
Exercise 8.2 — “In the stochastic theory, nothing evolves deterministically.
Exercise 8.3 — State the Markov property in one managerial sentence, and give one enterprise situation where it is a good declaration and one where it is not—identifying, in the second, the state enrichment (Chapter 5) that would restore it.
Exercise 8.4 — Using Example (see book), explain the difference between the mean path and a typical path, and why “the plan was right on average” and “the venture failed” can both be true.
Exercise 8.5 — Distinguish uncertainty, noise, and risk as this book reserves the terms, and state which chapter owns each.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 8.6 — From the definition of Brownian motion alone, derive \(\E[W(t)] = 0\), \(\Var(W(t)) = t\), and \(\Cov(W(s), W(t)) = \min(s, t)\) (hint: for \(s < t\) write \(W(t) = W(s) + (W(t) - W(s))\) and use independent increments).
Exercise 8.7 ★ —  Complete the proof of Lemma (see book): state the monotone class theorem you invoke, verify its hypotheses for the class of \(g\) satisfying the identity, and explain where boundedness of \(g\) is used.
Exercise 8.8 — For the two-state Markov chain with kernel \(P = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{smallmatrix}\bigr]\) (states: on-plan, off-plan), verify Chapman–Kolmogorov numerically (\(P^3 = P^2 P = P P^2\)), compute the stationary distribution, and interpret its entries.
Exercise 8.9 — Fill in the two steps quoted from [8] in the proof of Theorem (see book)(i): show that independent Gaussian vectors are jointly Gaussian, and that affine images of Gaussians are Gaussian, using characteristic functions.
Exercise 8.10 — For \(\mathbf{A} = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} 0.8 & 0.2 \\ 0 & 0.6 \end{smallmatrix}\bigr]\), \(\mathbf{G}\mathbf{Q} \mathbf{G}^{\!\top} = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} 0.04 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.01 \end{smallmatrix}\bigr]\), solve the discrete Lyapunov equation for \(\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{\infty}\) (set up the three scalar equations and solve), and verify against a truncation of the series.
Exercise 8.11 ★ —  Complete the uniqueness proof in Theorem (see book)(i): show carefully that a right-continuous survival function satisfying \(S(s + t) = S(s)S(t)\) with \(S(0) = 1\) and \(0 < S(1) < 1\) must be \(e^{-\lambda t}\), treating the rational and irrational steps separately.
Exercise 8.12 — Prove the law of total variance as used in Theorem (see book)(ii)—the identity of Theorem (see book)(iii)—directly from the tower property, and then apply it to decompose the variance of Example (see book)’s trough into within-jump-scenario and between-jump-scenario components (formulas only).
Exercise 8.13 — Derive the Monte Carlo standard error: show \(\Var(\hat\theta_N) = \Var(\varphi)/N\) for i.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 8.14 — (With AXIOM-08 or the Chapter 8 notebook.
Exercise 8.15 — (With AXIOM-08 or the Chapter 8 notebook.
Exercise 8.16 — (With AXIOM-08 or the Chapter 8 notebook.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 8.17 — Extend your Exercise 7.
Exercise 8.18 — Write the covenant-risk memo of Example (see book) for a threshold and persistence you estimate for your own enterprise (or Meridian variant): stationary volatility budget, per-period breach probability, and the persistence-versus-shock management levers, one page.
Exercise 8.19 — Conduct a jump audit for one event risk you know (regulatory, credit, key-person): estimate \(\lambda\) and the size moments from whatever record exists (state your method), compute the two ledger lines of Theorem (see book), and write the memorylessness paragraph for your board—or argue, explicitly, why your risk’s clock is not exponential and what you declare instead.

title: Chapter 9 · Enterprise State Architecture
text: Volume I, Chapter 9 · Taught in Week 5 · Lab seed 26109

title: Chapter 9 · Enterprise State Architecture
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Define the Enterprise State Architecture mathematically as the triple $\AS = (\Xc, \mathcal{R}, \mathcal{C})$.Construct enterprise state architectures for complex organizations: components, interfaces, wiring, and constraints, declared to contract standard.Identify state hierarchies and state dependencies, including the intrinsic causal hierarchy every dependency structure owns.Represent enterprise subsystems within a unified state architecture, and reassemble global behavior from wired components.Analyze enterprise structural consistency: interface, constraint, and hierarchical consistency, and the exact role of cycles in defeating local checks.Model interactions among enterprise states through the dependency graph, with propagation bounds along its paths.Evaluate architectural completeness relative to declared question classes.Interpret enterprise architectures graphically and mathematically, including the one-directional trust rule for aggregated dependency maps.Assess enterprise structural complexity with graph measures, and know what such measures do not measure.Prepare enterprise state architectures for the capital, performance, and risk architectures and their integration within UETA.

title: Chapter 9 · Enterprise State Architecture
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 9.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 9.1 — Distinguish representation from architecture in two sentences, and give one enterprise question answerable from the representation alone and one requiring the architecture (the wiring), justifying each.
Exercise 9.2 — For an enterprise you know, list six dependencies and classify each by Table (see book)’s types; identify one edge carrying multiple types and state which type each of two named analyses would read.
Exercise 9.3 — Explain the terminological note in Definition (see book): what does “topology” mean here, what did it mean in Chapter 3, and what single idea do the usages share?
Exercise 9.4 — Using Meridian (Example (see book)), explain in one paragraph how the intrinsic causal hierarchy can disagree with the organizational hierarchy and why the disagreement predicts coordination cost.
Exercise 9.5 — State in plain language why three policies can be pairwise compatible and jointly infeasible, and name the graph property that makes this possible and the one that forbids it.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 9.6 — Write out in full the proof that the condensation is acyclic (Proposition (see book)), including the composition-of- paths step, and exhibit a \(6\)-node digraph with exactly two nontrivial SCCs whose condensation has depth \(3\).
Exercise 9.7 — Re-prove the propagation bound (Theorem (see book)(ii)) with all induction details: state the induction hypothesis as a set equality of unaffected components, verify the in-neighbor distance inequality, and identify exactly where the proof uses that component updates read only in-neighbors.
Exercise 9.8 ★ —  Complete the activation splice of Theorem (see book)(iii): given a path \(u = w_0 \to \cdots \to w_m = v\) with each edge’s dependence active at some state configuration, construct a single configuration and horizon at which perturbing \(u\) alters \(v\), stating the additional hypothesis you need on the ability to steer intermediate blocks (and exhibiting a two-edge example where, without it, the influences cancel).
Exercise 9.9 — For the spurious-path counterexample of Theorem (see book)(ii), determine the minimal conditions under which a coarse edge \(A \to B\) does certify some fine edge: characterize the aggregates \(A\) for which coarse paths through \(A\) are never spurious (hint: internal connectivity from every in-interface node to every out-interface node).
Exercise 9.10 — Complete Theorem (see book)(ii)’s induction as a formal leaf-elimination argument: define arc-consistency projection onto the parent, show projections of arc-consistent families remain arc-consistent on the reduced tree, and conclude by induction on \(\abs{V}\); then explain in one sentence why the same argument fails on a cycle.
Exercise 9.11 — Construct a four-constraint family on a \(4\)-cycle of scalar components, all constraints of the form \(x_i - x_j \le b_{ij}\), that is arc-consistent and globally infeasible; state the general infeasibility criterion for difference constraints on a cycle (sum of bounds around the cycle) and verify it on your instance.
Exercise 9.12 — Prove that the projection of Theorem (see book)(i) is functorial for walks: concatenation of fine walks projects to concatenation of coarse walks, and lengths never increase; deduce \(d'([u], [v]) \le d(u, v)\) formally.
Exercise 9.13 ★ —  Magnitude along paths (first synthesis with Part II): suppose each component update is \(L\)-Lipschitz in each in-neighbor block and nonexpansive in its own.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 9.14 — (With AXIOM-09 or the Chapter 9 notebook.
Exercise 9.15 — (With AXIOM-09 or the Chapter 9 notebook.
Exercise 9.16 — (With AXIOM-09 or the Chapter 9 notebook.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 9.17 — Write the one-page ESA declaration for an enterprise you know: \(6\)–\(10\) components with spaces and interfaces, the typed edge list with one witnessing scenario per edge, three scoped constraints, and the intrinsic layering; flag every non-edge you are least confident in as an invariance claim to test.
Exercise 9.18 — Write a quarantine memo: for one shock source and one protected component in your Exercise (see book) architecture, list all directed paths between them, identify the smallest set of edges whose removal severs every path, and translate each removed edge into a managerial action with its cost in lost coordination (informal min-cut; the optimization is Volume II).
Exercise 9.19 — Conduct a constraint audit in the style of Example (see book): draw \(G_{\mathcal{C}}\) for eight or more real policies/obligations, compute \(\beta\), list the cycles, and propose the joint-review calendar the cycles require—one meeting per cycle, memberships attached.

title: Chapter 10 · Enterprise Capital Architecture
text: Volume I, Chapter 10 · Taught in Week 5 · Lab seed 26110

title: Chapter 10 · Enterprise Capital Architecture
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Define enterprise capital mathematically: durable, accumulable, depreciable, productive stocks carried on the state architecture.Distinguish among the forms of enterprise capital by their formal characteristics---depreciation, build time, tradability, separability---not by label alone.Construct enterprise capital vectors as declared measurements on the ESA, with carriers, units, and indicators.Analyze capital interactions: conversion channels with efficiencies, and complementarities with their bottleneck and supermodularity consequences.Evaluate capital dependencies through the capital dependency graph, inheriting the propagation discipline of Chapter 9.Measure the capital architecture: stocks, flows, productivity, and the conservation audit that detects undeclared channels.Explain capital accumulation exactly: geometric dynamics, the sustaining-investment identity, and time-to-build floors.Explain capital depletion and its asymmetry with accumulation---fast to burn, slow to rebuild---and its effect on the executable operator set.Interpret enterprise capital dynamics as Part II dynamics on a distinguished sub-state, with allocation as the control.Prepare capital representations for performance attribution (Chapter 11), risk (Chapter 12), and optimization (Chapter 15 and Volume II).

title: Chapter 10 · Enterprise Capital Architecture
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 10.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 10.1 — State the four stock-semantics properties of Definition (see book) and test each against three candidate “capitals”: a brand, a quarterly revenue figure, and an exclusive license; classify each as capital, flow, or neither, with one sentence per test.
Exercise 10.2 — Using Table (see book), explain why organizational capital’s profile (untradable, non-separable, low \(\delta\), long build) makes it simultaneously the most defensible and the most dangerous form to deplete.
Exercise 10.3 — Give one enterprise situation each for the bottleneck regime and the complementarity regime of Theorem (see book), and name the observable that would tell you which regime you are in.
Exercise 10.4 — Explain “capital is a rental” to a budget committee in four sentences, including the identity \(I = \delta C^{\ast}\) and one concrete consequence of a one-year rent holiday.
Exercise 10.5 — What is the plug of Theorem (see book)(iii), why is a persistent plug informative rather than embarrassing, and what two failures does a nonzero plug disambiguate between?


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 10.6 — Prove Theorem (see book)(i) by direct induction, then re-derive it as a corollary of Theorem 7.
Exercise 10.7 — Derive the time-to-build floor in full: from the gap recursion \(\bar C - C_{k+1} \ge (1-\delta)(\bar C - C_k)\), prove no admissible policy exceeds the full-throttle path at any date, and compute \(k_{0.1}\) for \(\delta \in \{0.05, 0.15, 0.30\}\).
Exercise 10.8 — Extend Theorem (see book)(ii) to chains: for a conversion path \(1 \to 2 \to \cdots \to m\) executed sequentially, show the end-to-end efficiency is \(\prod \eta_{i,i+1}\) and the total leak of converting amount \(T\) is \(T(1 - \prod \eta)\); relate the product form to Theorem 6.
Exercise 10.9 ★ —  State and prove the equalization conditions of Theorem (see book)(i) as the KKT conditions of the concave program \(\max F(\Cvec + \mathbf{a})\) s.
Exercise 10.10 — For \(F = \min(C_1/1,\ C_2/2)\) with \(\Cvec_0 = (10, 30)\) and budget \(B = 20\): compute the optimal allocation, the capacity path as a function of budget spent, and the kink point where the bottleneck rotates; sketch capacity versus budget.
Exercise 10.11 ★ —  For \(F(C_1, C_2) = C_1^{\alpha} C_2^{\beta}\) with \(\alpha, \beta > 0\), \(\alpha + \beta < 1\): verify strict complementarity (compute the cross-partial), solve the equalization conditions explicitly for the optimal split of budget \(B\), and show the funded ratio \(a_1/a_2\) depends on the existing stocks—one-sided histories tilt optimal funding toward the neglected complement.
Exercise 10.12 — Formalize the asymmetry of Proposition (see book): for a stock at floor \(c\) suffering a write-off of size \(w\), show the mandatory repurchase period is \(k(w) \ge \ln\bigl((\bar C - c + w)/(\bar C - c)\bigr) / \ln(1/(1-\delta))\) and expand for small \(w\) to obtain the marginal repurchase time \(w / (\delta(\bar C - c))\) periods per unit written off.
Exercise 10.13 — Prove the localization claim of Theorem (see book)(iii): if exactly one channel is undeclared, show \(\pi_{i,k}\) equals that channel’s flow (with sign determined by its direction), and explain how the correlation of \(\pi\) with candidate drivers identifies the channel.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 10.14 — (With AXIOM-10 or the Chapter 10 notebook.
Exercise 10.15 — (With AXIOM-10 or the Chapter 10 notebook.
Exercise 10.16 — (With AXIOM-10 or the Chapter 10 notebook.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 10.17 — Construct the capital vector for an enterprise you know: seven or more forms with carriers, units, two indicators each (Table (see book) style), estimated \(\delta\) with one sentence of evidence, and error bars—then flag the form whose measurement you trust least and why it matters most.
Exercise 10.18 — Write the sustaining-investment memo: for three stocks from Exercise (see book), compute the rent \(\delta C^{\ast}\), compare against actual maintenance flows, and state which stocks are being silently liquidated—one page, ending with the single reallocation you would propose.
Exercise 10.19 — Audit one past or planned transformation as capital conversion: declare its channels (\(I\), \(O\), \(T\) with \(\eta\) estimates), compute the conversion bill, run the plug logic on the slow stocks (\(C_O\), \(C_R\)), and price the repurchase liability of Proposition (see book)—the pro-forma line the original business case omitted.

title: Chapter 11 · Enterprise Performance Architecture
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Define enterprise performance mathematically: flow functionals of the enterprise state and decisions, distinguished from the stocks of Chapter 10.Distinguish performance from enterprise value, and constructs from the indicators and metrics that measure them.Construct enterprise performance vectors with declared scales, indicators, and error models.Analyze multidimensional performance architectures on the ESA, with dependency structure inherited from Chapter 9.Measure effectiveness, efficiency, and productivity as distinct, well-defined quantities.Evaluate performance dependencies, including transformation as the coupling channel and the J-curve as a theorem.Interpret performance trajectories and fans: performance dynamics as Chapter 7--8 dynamics of a flow functional.Explain performance trade-offs exactly: Pareto structure, frontier slopes as exchange rates, and what weighted indices can and cannot find.Model performance under multiple objectives, with aggregation's theorems---rank reversal, normalization sensitivity, compensability---as working knowledge.Prepare performance models for the risk architecture (Chapter 12) and the optimization of Chapter 15 and Volume II.

title: Chapter 11 · Enterprise Performance Architecture
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 11.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 11.1 — Classify each as stock (Chapter 10) or flow (this chapter), one sentence each: engineering capability; quarterly ROIC; customer relationships; on-time delivery rate; the innovation pipeline; new-product revenue share.
Exercise 11.2 — For two dimensions of Table (see book), name the construct, one indicator, and one way the indicator’s non-injectivity (Theorem (see book)(iii)) could lose a preference before any gaming occurs.
Exercise 11.3 — Explain to a board in three sentences why, between two options neither of which dominates, “the scorecard says A” is a statement about the weights, citing which clause of Theorem (see book) you are invoking.
Exercise 11.4 — State the evaluation-window doctrine of Theorem (see book)(iii) in plain language, and give one real program class where the window is habitually shorter than the build lag.
Exercise 11.5 — Distinguish delivered performance from sustainable performance (Definition (see book)) and explain what “borrowed level” borrows from, in Chapter 10’s terms.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 11.6 ★ —  Write out the additive-index characterization (Theorem (see book)(i)) in full: the coordinate decomposition, the rational case of Cauchy’s equation, and the monotone squeeze; then exhibit why dropping monotonicity admits pathological solutions (state the role of a Hamel basis without constructing one).
Exercise 11.7 — For Example (see book)’s options \(A\) and \(B\), compute the tie locus in the weight simplex restricted to \(\mathbf{w} = (w, w, \tfrac{1-2w}{3}, \tfrac{1-2w}{3}, \tfrac{1-2w}{3})\): find the \(w^{\ast}\) at which the ranking flips, verifying Theorem (see book)(ii)’s intermediate-value clause.
Exercise 11.8 — Construct your own normalization reversal: two non-dominated alternatives on two dimensions and two normalizations (min–max with two different reference ranges) such that equal weights rank them oppositely; verify with Theorem (see book)(iii)’s effective-weight reading.
Exercise 11.9 — Build a three-division Simpson instance: all three margins improve, group margin falls; then prove the safe rule in general —the pooled ratio equals the mix-weighted average of component ratios, and exhibit the mix-effect/rate-effect bridge decomposition.
Exercise 11.10 — Extend the J-curve to a \(K\)-period ledger with geometric build decay: \(x_{k+1} = (1 - \delta)x_k + b u_k\), program \(u_1 = \bar{u}\) only.
Exercise 11.11 — Prove Theorem (see book)(ii) for weak Pareto optimality under nonnegative (not strictly positive) weights, and exhibit the gap: a weakly-but-not-strictly Pareto point selected by a weighting with a zero weight.
Exercise 11.12 ★ —  Generalize Theorem (see book)(iii): for the discrete set of alternatives \((0, m)\), \((a, a)\), and \((m, 0)\), determine exactly the pairs \((a, m)\) for which the balanced point is Pareto optimal yet unsupported, and interpret the boundary case geometrically (collinearity).
Exercise 11.13 — Carry out the tangency computation of Theorem (see book)(iv) on the concrete frontier \(p_1^2 + 2p_2^2 = 100\): find the supported point for \(\mathbf{w} = (1, 1)\), verify the slope equals \(-1\) there, and compute the exchange rate at the point selected by \(\mathbf{w} = (1, 3)\).


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 11.14 — (With AXIOM-11 or the Chapter 11 notebook.
Exercise 11.15 — (With AXIOM-11 or the Chapter 11 notebook.
Exercise 11.16 — (With AXIOM-11 or the Chapter 11 notebook.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 11.17 — Write the one-page EPA declaration for an enterprise you know: six or more dimensions with supports on your Exercise 9.
Exercise 11.18 — Audit one real scorecard for ratio aggregation: identify every ratio KPI that is averaged across units, test one for the Simpson condition with actual or realistic numbers, and draft the pooled-ratio-plus-mix-bridge replacement exhibit.
Exercise 11.19 — Map a real target cascade as a constraint family: draw the coupling graph across six or more units/functions, run the tree-versus-cycle audit of Theorem (see book)(ii), and where you find a cycle, specify the joint feasibility session—members, shared resource, and the single consolidated check that replaces the bilateral sign-offs.

title: Chapter 12 · Enterprise Risk, Resilience, and Robustness Architecture
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Define enterprise risk mathematically: the exposure of declared objectives to the state distribution, evaluated by declared risk functionals.Distinguish risk, uncertainty, robustness, and resilience as four different mathematical objects answering four different questions.Construct enterprise risk vectors with declared loss channels and architectural supports.Model enterprise vulnerabilities as damage maps on the architecture.Analyze resilience and recoverability through basin geometry and contraction rates, including recovery to the volatility band rather than to a point.Evaluate enterprise robustness: worst-case guarantees, robustness radii, and the price of the worst case.Measure enterprise survivability: survival functions, hazards, and the race between shock arrival and recovery.Understand systemic enterprise risk: gain-weighted propagation, cascade thresholds, and common-shock dependence.Integrate uncertainty into architectural analysis: stress equilibria, reverse stress tests, and the audit framework.Prepare the risk architecture for unification in UETA and optimization under uncertainty in Volume II.

title: Chapter 12 · Enterprise Risk, Resilience, and Robustness Architecture
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 12.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 12.1 — Using Table (see book), classify each of the following statements by which member of the quartet it invokes, rewriting any that conflate two: “we can absorb a $20”there is a $5 breach at the identity provider would take out three systems”; “we were back inside plan range within four quarters.
Exercise 12.2 — Explain the Knightian split (Definition (see book)) in two sentences, give one enterprise channel you would treat with a declared law and one with only a set, and justify each choice.
Exercise 12.3 — “Choosing between VaR and expected shortfall is a technical detail for the risk team.
Exercise 12.4 — A post-crisis report tracks distance to the pre-shock operating point and concludes “recovery incomplete” eight quarters running.
Exercise 12.5 — Explain, without formulas, the two distinct reasons systemic risk exceeds the sum of marginal risks (Theorem (see book)(iv)), with one enterprise instance of each.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 12.6 — Construct your own VaR subadditivity failure at the $99 choose loss probabilities and sizes for two independent exposures so that each has $_{0.
Exercise 12.7 — Prove the minimax inequality of Theorem (see book)(ii) in full, and construct a \(2 \times 2\) decision–disturbance table with a strict gap; interpret the gap as the value of foresight about the disturbance.
Exercise 12.8 — Derive the recovery clock (see book) from the contraction bound, including the ceiling; then derive the first-order sensitivity \(\partial k_{\varepsilon}/\partial \bar\rho \approx k_{\varepsilon}/(1 - \bar\rho)\) near \(\bar\rho = 1\) and interpret the divergence.
Exercise 12.9 — Verify Theorem (see book)(ii)’s construction: compute both recovery clocks for a shock \(s = 0.5\) at \(\varepsilon = 0.05\), exhibit a shock size where the rankings reverse, and prove that no scalar score \(\sigma(m, \bar\rho)\), strictly monotone in each argument, can rank both comparisons consistently.
Exercise 12.10 ★ —  Complete the induction of Theorem (see book)(ii): state the entrywise vector induction precisely, justify the triangle-inequality step from the componentwise Lipschitz hypothesis, and show where nonexpansiveness in a component’s own state is used (exhibit a counterexample bound when it fails).
Exercise 12.11 — Prove the Neumann equivalence of Theorem (see book)(iii): convergence of \(\sum_j \mathbf{G}^j\) iff \(\rho(\mathbf{G}) < 1\), using Theorem 7.
Exercise 12.12 — Prove the hazard factorization and the expected-survival identity \(\E[\text{failure epoch}] = \sum_{k \ge 0} S(k)\) of Theorem (see book)(i), and evaluate both for stationary $h = 1.6 quarter survival under the stressed hazard $9.
Exercise 12.13 ★ —  Generalize the common-shock construction of Theorem (see book)(iv): for common-cause probability \(z\) and idiosyncratic probability \(q\) per unit, derive the exact joint failure probability and the ratio to the independence estimate; show the ratio is maximized as \(q \to 0\) and compute its limit \(1/z\); interpret.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 12.14 — (With AXIOM-12 or the Chapter 12 notebook.
Exercise 12.15 — (With AXIOM-12 or the Chapter 12 notebook.
Exercise 12.16 — (With AXIOM-12 or the Chapter 12 notebook.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 12.17 — Construct the risk vector for an enterprise you know: eight or more channels of Table (see book) with loss channel, architectural support (from your Exercise 9.
Exercise 12.18 — Audit one real stress-testing exercise you have seen against Table (see book): score each row present/absent/partial, identify whether a reverse stress test (robustness radius) was run, and write the two-paragraph gap memo.
Exercise 12.19 — Write the race memo for one recurring shock channel: estimate \(\lambda\) from the record, compute \(T_{\mathrm{rec}}\) from your enterprise’s observed recovery episodes (state the tolerance used), report \(1 - e^{-\lambda T_{\mathrm{rec}}}\), and propose one lever on each of \(\lambda\), \(T_{\mathrm{rec}}\), and margin with rough costs.

title: Chapter 13 · Unified Enterprise Transformation Architecture
text: Volume I, Chapter 13 · Taught in Week 6 · Lab seed 26113

title: Chapter 13 · Unified Enterprise Transformation Architecture
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Explain why enterprise transformation cannot be understood from any single architecture, and why unification is a mathematical necessity rather than a presentational convenience.Integrate the five enterprise architectures mathematically on their shared skeleton, and state exactly what the integration adds and what it leaves untouched.Construct Unified Enterprise Transformation Architectures for declared enterprises: skeleton, layers, couplings, declarations.Analyze interactions among architectures through the layered graph and the enterprise interaction matrix.Evaluate architectural consistency across layers, including the cross-layer cycles that defeat local audits.Model enterprise evolution in UETA: one state equation, five synchronized ledgers.Analyze architectural dependencies: who reads what, who writes what, and along which certified channels.Represent enterprise transformation holistically: a single program evaluated simultaneously in state, operator, capital, performance, and risk terms.Prepare unified enterprise models for mathematical analysis (Chapter 14) and optimization (Chapter 15).Understand the theoretical foundation that UETA provides for the General Enterprise Optimization Problem.

title: Chapter 13 · Unified Enterprise Transformation Architecture
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 13.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 13.1 — State the three integration principles of Section (see book) in your own words, and for each, name the theorem clause of this chapter that makes it exact.
Exercise 13.2 — “Our transformation office evaluates programs on NPV; capital and risk sign off separately.
Exercise 13.3 — A divisional CFO objects that unification will “override our capital model.
Exercise 13.4 — For each zero block a governance team might declare in \(\mathbf{M}\) (say \(\AR \to \AT\): risk never restricts operator menus), state what the declaration certifies, what would void it, and who should be accountable for its honesty (Theorem (see book)(iii)).
Exercise 13.5 — Explain, without formulas, why an enterprise that funds transformation from its stocks, manages to a plan corridor, and declares funded buffers necessarily has a constraint cycle that bilateral audits cannot certify (Theorem (see book)(iii)), and give the double-counted quantity in the canonical failure.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 13.6 — Write out the trivial unification of Proposition (see book) for a two-component enterprise (operators, capital map, one performance dimension, one risk functional, \(\mathcal{K} = \varnothing\)), verify (C1)–(C5) explicitly, and identify the first question that forces a nonempty \(\mathcal{K}\).
Exercise 13.7 — Complete the separating constructions of Theorem (see book)(iv): for the \(\AP\) and \(\AC\) cases, give explicit numerical instances (two declarations each) and the question whose answer separates them.
Exercise 13.8 ★ — Formalize integration as a universal construction: show that \(\AS \boxplus L\) satisfies a pushout-style universal property over the skeleton (any object receiving compatible maps from the layers factors through the UETA uniquely), and derive Theorem (see book)(i) from it.
Exercise 13.9 — Two divisional UETAs share a treasury component but declare its funding-capacity indicator under different measurement contracts.
Exercise 13.10 — Prove the principal-submatrix inequality used in Theorem (see book)(ii) (for nonnegative \(\mathbf{M}\), \(\rho(\mathbf{M}_{\ell\ell}) \le \rho(\mathbf{M})\)), then generalize the gap construction: for any \(\varepsilon > 0\), build \(\mathbf{M}\) with all diagonal radii \(\le \varepsilon\) and \(\rho(\mathbf{M}) \ge 1\).
Exercise 13.11 — For the Meridian layered graph of Example (see book), prove that layered distances weakly exceed skeleton distances between paired nodes, and characterize exactly when the inequality is strict (as in the covenant’s \(3\) versus \(2\)).
Exercise 13.12 — Prove the deterministic case of Theorem (see book)(ii) directly (point-mass kernel), then show by a two-point example with a strictly convex loss functional that replacing the law by its mean breaks the square, quantifying the gap via Jensen.
Exercise 13.13 ★ — Make Theorem (see book)(v) sharp: construct a UETA in which an invariant violation is consistent with errors in two different layers within one layered SCC, and show no observation of readouts outside the SCC can distinguish them— then give the minimal additional probe that can.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 13.14 — (AXIOM-13) Build the Meridian interaction matrix from Table (see book)’s standing gains, compute \(\rho(\mathbf{M})\) and its block attribution, and find the smallest single-block gain increase that crosses the cascade threshold.
Exercise 13.15 — (AXIOM-13) The consistency auditor’s preloaded instance: verify computationally that each pairwise audit passes and the joint feasibility check fails; then repair the instance three ways (relax the buffer, the corridor, the funding) and compare the repairs’ costs in the performance ledger.
Exercise 13.16 — (AXIOM-13) Seed the digital program at \((\mathrm{DIG}, T)\) and a demand shock at \((\mathrm{MKT}, S)\) in the same quarter; simulate the layered wavefronts and produce the ledger-clock table: first admissible quarter of effect for every (component, layer) readout.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 13.17 — Take an enterprise you know and draft its coupling register: at least one channel per class of Table (see book), each with carrier, direction, and a defensible gain estimate.
Exercise 13.18 — For the bank of Example (see book), write the three declarations that read regulatory capital (stock, buffer, denominator) as one skeleton quantity under (C2)–(C3), and construct a concrete pairwise-consistent, jointly-infeasible instance among the three functions.
Exercise 13.19 — Reconstruct Example (see book)’s three separated verdicts as constrained versions of the unified problem (Proposition (see book)(ii)): state exactly what each office holds fixed, exhibit the active coupling its verdict ignores, and rank the three separation gaps using the standing Meridian numbers.

title: Chapter 14 · Mathematical Analysis of the Unified Enterprise Transformation Architecture
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Analyze the mathematical properties of unified enterprise systems: regularity, compactness, coupling, and the assumption panel that makes analysis honest.Establish the analytical consistency conditions under which the unified object is a well-posed dynamic system.Evaluate enterprise stability: spectral tests, Lyapunov certificates, certified stability budgets, and the coupling-destabilization phenomenon.Analyze enterprise feasibility beyond the single period: feasible regions, viability kernels, and recursive feasibility.Investigate enterprise sensitivity: variational dynamics, steady-state multipliers, and conditioning near criticality.Characterize enterprise equilibria: existence, uniqueness, location in the feasible region, and their response to transformation.Prove the principal analytical results governing unified enterprise evolution, invoking Parts II--III without re-derivation.Interpret each analytical property as a governance quantity with an owner, a monitor, and a failure mode.Evaluate structural robustness: how spectra, sensitivities, and feasible regions respond to structural perturbation.Prepare unified enterprise models for optimization: the assumptions, conditions, and derivative objects the GEOP consumes.

title: Chapter 14 · Mathematical Analysis of the Unified Enterprise Transformation Architecture
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 14.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 14.1 — For each row of the assumption panel (Table (see book)), name the enterprise office that owns it, one way it fails in practice, and one theorem of this chapter that dies with it.
Exercise 14.2 — “Our model produces a forecast, so the plan exists.
Exercise 14.3 — A group CFO proposes certifying enterprise stability by collecting stability attestations from the five architecture owners.
Exercise 14.4 — Explain, without formulas, why the same number governs how fast an enterprise recovers from shocks and how violently it responds to recalibrations (Theorem (see book)(iii)), and what this implies for change management during integrations (Example (see book)).
Exercise 14.5 — Give two operational consequences of backward non-uniqueness (Theorem (see book)(iv)): one for audit design and one for dispute resolution over “what caused the current state.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 14.6 — Prove the unified Lipschitz assembly claim of Proposition (see book): for \(f\) built from layer maps by composition and superposition, exhibit the unified constant \(L\) in terms of the layer constants, and give an example where the bound is tight.
Exercise 14.7 — Prove Theorem (see book)(ii) in full, then extend it to time-varying parameters \(\theta_k\) with \(\sum_k \norm{\theta_{k+1} - \theta_k} < \infty\), bounding the trajectory drift by the total declaration variation.
Exercise 14.8 ★ — Prove the norm construction used in Theorem (see book)(i): for any matrix \(\Jac\) and any \(r > \rho(\Jac)\) there exists a vector norm whose induced matrix norm satisfies \(\norm{\Jac} \le r\).
Exercise 14.9 — Prove the block bound \(\rho(\Jac) \le \rho(\widehat{\mathbf{M}})\) of Theorem (see book)(iii), and construct a two-layer system where the diagonal blocks have radius \(0.5\), the block small-gain test fails, and the unified system is unstable.
Exercise 14.10 — Derive the steady-state multiplier \(\mathbf{S}_{\infty} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}\) three ways: as the fixed point of (see book), by the implicit function theorem on \(\x^{*} = f(\x^{*};\theta)\), and by summing the walk expansion; verify the three agree on a \(2 \times 2\) example with one cross-layer channel.
Exercise 14.11 — Prove the Perron-direction comparison of Theorem (see book)(iii), and show by example that for non-normal \(\mathbf{A}\) the transient sensitivity \(\norm{\mathbf{S}_k}\) can exceed the steady-state \(\norm{\mathbf{S}_{\infty}}\) before settling.
Exercise 14.12 ★ — Prove that the viability iteration of Theorem (see book)(iii) converges to the kernel under A1–A2 with a compact admissible decision set, including the finite-intersection selection argument, and exhibit a system where \(V_j \ne V_{j+1}\) for all \(j\) (the kernel is reached only in the limit).
Exercise 14.13 — Prove the discrete invariance principle (Theorem (see book)(ii)) in full from the limit-set properties of trajectories under continuous maps on compact sets, and derive clause (i)’s global convergence as a corollary with \(V(\x) = \norm{\x - \x^{*}}\) in the contraction norm.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 14.14 — (AXIOM-14) On the Meridian stability bench, find the single-block gain increase with the largest destabilizing effect per unit of gain (the spectral sensitivity of \(\rho\) to blocks), compare with the interaction-matrix answer of Exercise (see book), and explain any difference.
Exercise 14.15 — (AXIOM-14) Run the viability iteration on the restructuring corridor of Example (see book); report the emptying horizon, then implement both repairs and report the reopened kernels’ volumes and the repairs’ ledger costs.
Exercise 14.16 — (AXIOM-14) Continue the equilibrium \(\x^{*}(\theta)\) along the digital program’s schedule (Example (see book)); verify \(\Delta\x^{*} \approx \mathbf{S}_{\infty}\Delta\theta\) for small steps, locate where the linear prediction degrades, and relate the degradation to the Hessian flag of Theorem (see book)(v).


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 14.17 — Design the gain audit of Theorem (see book)(ii) for an enterprise you know: data sources for estimating three Jacobian blocks, the comparison procedure against declared ceilings, the escalation path for a violation, and the localization argument you would use.
Exercise 14.18 — For a publicized acquisition in your industry, reconstruct Example (see book)’s panel qualitatively: which blocks of the combined gain matrix were new, what the integration plan’s milestones were in gain-reduction terms, and one observed symptom consistent with a collapsed spectral gap.
Exercise 14.19 — Set up the monotone bracketing of Theorem (see book)(iii) for a planning cycle: specify conservative and optimistic declarations, state the order-preservation check on the Jacobian sign pattern, identify one coupling in your enterprise that would break it, and describe what the twin books certify while it holds.

title: Chapter 15 · Mathematical Formulation of Enterprise Optimization
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Formulate the General Enterprise Optimization Problem in canonical form and instantiate it for a declared enterprise.Identify enterprise decision variables and distinguish decisions from controls, policies, and programs.Define enterprise objective functions and value functionals from the ledgers of Parts II--IV.Construct enterprise constraint systems across the nine declared classes and audit their consistency.Formulate multi-objective enterprise optimization problems and interpret their scalarizations.Distinguish static, dynamic, multi-period, stochastic, robust, and adaptive formulations and know when each is the honest one.Analyze feasible enterprise solution spaces, including their viability-honest cores.Interpret Pareto-optimal enterprises and efficiency frontiers as governance objects.Evaluate enterprise trade-offs through multipliers, shadow prices, and frontier slopes.Prepare enterprise models for the solution methodologies of Volume II without committing to any of them.

title: Chapter 15 · Mathematical Formulation of Enterprise Optimization
text:
Exercises
24 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 15.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 15.1 — For each of the three inherited frameworks criticized in Section (see book) (portfolio selection, capacity planning, valuation maximization), state precisely which blocks of (see book)–(see book) it freezes, per Theorem (see book)(ii).
Exercise 15.2 — Build the decision inventory of Table (see book) for an enterprise you know: three declared levers with owners and admissible bands, and one commonly assumed lever that fails the declaration test—say why.
Exercise 15.3 — Take one KPI and write its four declarations per Table (see book) (objective component, constraint, policy input, monitor).
Exercise 15.4 — Express the difference between the stochastic, robust, and adaptive GEOPs as three one-sentence promises to a board, and state for each the declaration object that backs the promise.
Exercise 15.5 — A consultancy proposes to “optimize first and patch feasibility after.
Exercise 15.6 — Explain, without formulas, why the holdable optimum of Theorem (see book)(iii) can be strictly worse on paper yet strictly better as a plan, and which two Chapter 14 certificates it is buying.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 15.7 — Carry out the \(K = 1\) and static-lift reductions of Theorem (see book)(ii) and Theorem (see book)(iii) in full for a two-period instance: exhibit the lifted variables, constraints, and the identification of the dynamics’ multipliers with the costates.
Exercise 15.8 ★ — Prove the Farkas alternative used in Theorem (see book)(i) for polyhedral cones, and derive the KKT conditions (see book) from it under the Slater grade, including complementary slackness.
Exercise 15.9 — Prove Theorem (see book)(iii) (the shadow-price derivative) from the perturbed value function’s concavity, and compute the covenant multiplier in a two-dimensional capital-allocation instance with explicit data.
Exercise 15.10 — Prove both directions of Theorem (see book)(ii), including the supporting-hyperplane converse under convexity, and construct a three-point nonconvex attainable set whose middle frontier point no weighted sum finds.
Exercise 15.11 — Prove Theorem (see book)(iii) and its weak converse (maximizers of \(\varepsilon\)-constraint instances are weakly Pareto optimal; strengthen to Pareto optimal under uniqueness).
Exercise 15.12 — Prove the maximum-theorem step of Theorem (see book)(i) in full for the finite horizon: upper hemicontinuity and compact-valuedness of the feasible decision correspondence, continuity of \(V_k\), and attainment.
Exercise 15.13 ★ — For the coordination result of Theorem (see book)(ii): state and prove strong duality for the partially relaxed convex GEOP under the Slater grade, show the coordinated block optima at \(\boldsymbol{\lambda}_c^{*}\) solve the unified problem, and bound the duality gap when convexity fails, relating it to the value of coordination.
Exercise 15.14 — Prove Theorem (see book)(ii): the Bellman operator’s contraction, uniqueness of the fixed point, geometric convergence of value iteration with a priori and a posteriori bounds, and existence of an optimal stationary selection.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 15.15 — (AXIOM-15) Assemble the capital-allocation instance of Example (see book) in the GEOP builder; run both audits; report the binding set, all multipliers, and the…
Exercise 15.16 — (AXIOM-15) Trace the growth–trough frontier by weight sweep and by \(\varepsilon\)-constraint; locate the knee and the segment the weight sweep misses; report frontier slopes at three declared points and reconcile them with the instances’ multipliers.
Exercise 15.17 — (AXIOM-15) Reproduce the turnaround comparison of Example (see book): certainty-equivalent, stochastic, and robust optima with their mutual gaps; then vary the ambiguity radius and plot the price of distrust.
Exercise 15.18 — (AXIOM-15) Run the roadmap instance of Example (see book) with and without the terminal viability and holdability constraints; report the two value gaps and exhibit the corner-trap trajectory the unconstrained window recommends.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 15.19 — Draft the one-page “priced commitments” exhibit for a real or case enterprise: five plausibly binding constraints with owners, the multiplier each would carry, and the decision each price would inform.
Exercise 15.20 — Prepare both ESG declarations of Example (see book) for a board: the ceiling form with its internal carbon price and the objective form with its frontier knee.
Exercise 15.21 — Identify a coupling in an enterprise you know that is currently “coordinated” by negotiation.
Exercise 15.22 — Design the measurement that would estimate the value of information for one dashboard upgrade: the two policy classes, the two GEOP instances, and the operational data needed to declare them honestly (Theorem (see book)(iii)).
Exercise 15.23 — For a publicized turnaround or restructuring, reconstruct the instance qualitatively: decisions, objective, the three most plausible binding classes, and whether the observed plan shows corner-trap symptoms (per-window optimality with shrinking room).
Exercise 15.24 ★ — Design a full multi-objective mandate for an enterprise of your choice: components with owners and units (Table (see book)), the chosen device of Table (see book) with justification, the frontier exhibit the board would see, and the governance protocol for moving along the frontier.

title: Chapter 16 · Synthesis, Future Directions, and Transition to Volume II
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Explain the complete Dynamic Corporate Transformation framework as one deductive structure.Describe the relationships among the six foundational architectures and the contracts that bind them.Summarize the General Enterprise Optimization Problem and its place as the volume's culmination.Identify the framework's unresolved theoretical questions and state them precisely.Explain the transition from enterprise architecture to enterprise optimization as a certified interface.Distinguish the scope of Volume I from that of Volume II and route questions to the correct volume.Identify research opportunities and formulate them as extensions of declared framework components.Describe the computational roadmap for the framework, from laboratory to digital twin.Understand the intended long-term evolution of the discipline, including its AI-assisted and autonomous horizons.Appreciate the interdisciplinary significance of Dynamic Corporate Transformation and locate it among its parent fields.
